name: SpamCrackers — Architecture Decision Record
description: Retrospectively recorded architecture decisions: the reasoning behind key choices, the alternatives rejected, and the trade-off each one accepts.
note: These records were written after the decisions were made; dates reflect when each was recorded, not necessarily when it was first taken.

decisions.title: Static site with client-side checks, no backend
decisions.context: The core tool grades a domain's email authentication. That could run on a server or in the browser.
decisions.decision: Ship a fully static site; run domain checks in the browser over DNS-over-HTTPS. No server, no accounts, no storage.

alternatives.option: A server API doing the DNS lookups
alternatives.rejectedBecause: Adds hosting, rate-limiting, logging and a privacy surface; a domain's grade and the visitor are never ours to hold if there is no server.
Unnecessary for content that is deterministic and cache-friendly; it would trade the privacy guarantee for marginal speed.

decisions.tradeoff: Checks are bounded to what public DNS over HTTPS and the browser allow — most notably the DKIM selector heuristic (ADR-0008). There is no place to persist per-user history.

decisions.title: Git history as the Observatory datastore
decisions.context: The Observatory needed to become a dated record, not a per-visit sample — which implies storage of measurements over time.
decisions.decision: Write each weekly measurement as JSON into the repository (latest.json + an immutable history/ archive). Git history is the audit trail.

alternatives.rejectedBecause: Introduces an external dependency, credentials and a retention policy to manage, for data that is small, append-mostly and benefits from being openly diffable.
Far heavier than the data warrants, and it would move the record out of the open, versioned artifact the rest of the asset already is.

decisions.tradeoff: Granularity is weekly, not real-time; there is no query API; and the repository grows over time (bounded — snapshots are ~2 KB and git-compressed). If sub-weekly or queryable history is ever needed, this decision would be revisited.

decisions.title: Deterministic build scripts over a framework
decisions.context: Pages are generated from data (techniques, patterns, records). Something has to turn data into HTML.
decisions.decision: Use small, dependency-free Node scripts that read data and emit complete HTML, verified by one gate.

alternatives.rejectedBecause: Adds a toolchain and dependency tree to maintain and audit, for output we can produce exactly with a few readable scripts.
Ships a runtime and build system to render content that is fundamentally static; it works against the privacy and longevity goals.

decisions.tradeoff: Componentisation is manual and there is no plugin ecosystem — shared markup is duplicated across builders and kept honest by the gate rather than by a template engine.

decisions.title: Permanent, structured identifiers
decisions.context: Techniques, patterns and decisions are cited by identifier. Citations must keep resolving.
decisions.decision: Assign structured, namespaced identifiers (e.g. SPO-AU-03, EAP-01, ADR-0001) that are permanent: never reused or renumbered; superseded entries are deprecated, not deleted.

alternatives.option: Human slugs as the primary key
alternatives.rejectedBecause: Slugs drift as wording is refined, breaking citations; they are good URLs but poor identifiers.
Loses the pillar/phase structure that makes an id self-describing and sortable.

decisions.tradeoff: Identifiers cannot be tidied or renumbered even when the set would look cleaner; gaps and deprecations are permanent. Durability is bought with a little untidiness.

decisions.title: Pillar pages are the source; model.json is generated
decisions.context: The technique corpus exists both as readable pages and as machine-readable model.json. One must be authoritative to avoid drift.
decisions.decision: Treat the built pillar pages as the source of record and derive model.json from them; the gate checks the JSON conforms and stays in sync.

alternatives.option: JSON as source, pages generated from it
alternatives.rejectedBecause: Equally valid, but it makes the human-readable, canonical artifact a by-product of a data file — the opposite of a reference whose pages are the citable object.

decisions.tradeoff: The JSON must be regenerated whenever page content changes, and that discipline is enforced by process and the gate rather than being impossible to get wrong.

decisions.title: One canonical Organization across the knowledge graph
decisions.context: Every page emits JSON-LD. Search and knowledge graphs resolve entities by identity.
decisions.decision: Define a single Organization entity and reference it by a shared @id on every page, rather than inlining it.

alternatives.option: Inline the Organization on each page
alternatives.rejectedBecause: Produces many near-duplicate entities that a consumer cannot reliably unify into one publisher.

decisions.tradeoff: Every page must carry the identical @id, and a publisher change means touching all of them — a constraint the gate enforces so it cannot silently diverge.

decisions.title: One deterministic gate as the definition of valid
decisions.context: The asset has many invariants: links, structured data, schema conformance, cross-artifact sync.
decisions.decision: Consolidate every invariant into one script (scripts/verify.js) that runs in CI and blocks merge on any failure.

alternatives.option: A collection of linters and ad-hoc checks
alternatives.rejectedBecause: Spreads the definition of correct across tools and configs; nothing owns the cross-artifact invariants that matter most here.
Not reproducible and does not scale to hundreds of links and several machine-readable datasets.

decisions.tradeoff: The gate is bespoke and must itself be maintained and trusted; a bug in it is a single point of failure — mitigated by keeping it small, and by negative-testing new invariants when they are added.

decisions.title: Two grading scores instead of one
decisions.context: DKIM cannot be read exactly from DNS without knowing the selector; SPF, DMARC and MX can.
decisions.decision: Keep two scores: the full four-control check (with a bounded DKIM selector heuristic) for a single domain, and a DKIM-agnostic three-control index, rescaled to 100, for the Observatory at scale.

alternatives.rejectedBecause: Either drags the DKIM heuristic into the at-scale index (introducing false negatives across many domains) or drops DKIM from the single-domain grade (losing a real control).

decisions.tradeoff: Two numbers exist for the same domain and must be reconciled; a perfect-but-no-DKIM domain reads A+ in the index and B in the full check. The reconciliation is stated explicitly rather than hidden.

decisions.title: Defensive-only scope as an absolute boundary
decisions.context: The intelligence model describes how email abuse works. Depth is valuable; operational detail is dangerous.
decisions.decision: Keep every entry at the level of classification and defence — what it is, how it is observed, how it is countered — with no payloads, kits or step-by-step methods, at every version, by policy.

alternatives.rejectedBecause: Would convert a defensive reference into an abuse enabler; it is out of scope regardless of demand.

decisions.tradeoff: Readers seeking offensive tradecraft will not find it here; that audience is deliberately unserved.

decisions.title: Security headers via meta-CSP plus edge rules
decisions.context: The site is hosted on GitHub Pages (which cannot set response headers) and fronted by Cloudflare (which can).
decisions.decision: Ship a meta http-equiv Content-Security-Policy in every page so protection holds regardless of host, and mirror the header set at the Cloudflare edge.

alternatives.rejectedBecause: Inert on GitHub Pages — it would give a false sense of protection with none applied.

alternatives.option: Move to a host that sets headers
alternatives.rejectedBecause: Trades the simplicity and durability of static hosting for a capability a meta-CSP already provides.

decisions.tradeoff: The policy lives in two places (page meta and edge rules) that must be kept aligned; the meta-CSP is the guaranteed floor, the edge rules the enhancement.
